skill.name: test-driven-development
skill.description: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
skill.summary: A methodology skill that guides AI agents to write tests first, then implement code to pass those tests
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate Test-Driven Development educational skill. The static analyzer detected 73 potential issues, but after semantic evaluation, all findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The 'external_commands' detections are markdown code block delimiters (backticks) in documentation, not actual shell execution. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'network reconnaissance' detections are similarly false positives triggered by keywords in educational context. No executable malicious code exists in this skill.

medium_findings.title: Markdown Code Block Delimiters Flagged as Shell Execution
medium_findings.description: The static scanner detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' patterns in markdown files. These are FALSE POSITIVES - the backticks are markdown code fence delimiters (```typescript, ```bash) used for syntax highlighting in documentation, not runtime command execution. The skill is educational content about TDD methodology with no actual shell commands being executed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Examined the actual file contents - backticks appear exclusively in markdown code fences for TypeScript and Bash examples demonstrating test code, not as shell execution operators

content.value_statement: Writing tests after code leads to false confidence and missed edge cases. This skill enforces the TDD discipline: write a failing test first, then implement just enough code to pass.
actual_capabilities: Guide the AI to write failing tests before any implementation code
Enforce the Red-Green-Refactor cycle with verification steps
Prevent common TDD violations like testing after implementation
Provide testing anti-patterns to avoid mock-focused tests
Teach minimal implementation - only write code that passes current tests
limitations: Does not execute tests - only provides methodology guidance
Requires user to run test commands manually
Does not generate specific test cases for particular codebases
Assumes user has testing framework already configured

use_cases.title: New Feature Implementation
use_cases.description: When building a new feature, write the test first that defines the expected behavior, watch it fail, then implement to make it pass
use_cases.target_user: Software developers wanting to practice TDD

use_cases.title: Bug Fix Workflow
use_cases.description: When fixing a bug, first write a test that reproduces the bug, watch it fail, then fix the code to make it pass

use_cases.title: Refactoring with Confidence
use_cases.description: Before refactoring, ensure tests exist. The tests verify the behavior remains correct after code changes

prompt_templates.title: Start TDD for New Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to implement [describe feature]. Following TDD, first write a failing test that defines the expected behavior. Don't write implementation code yet.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginning a new feature with test-first approach

prompt_templates.title: Verify Red Phase
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the test I just wrote and confirm it fails for the right reason (feature missing, not due to typos). Show me the exact failure message.
prompt_templates.scenario: Confirming the test fails correctly before implementation

prompt_templates.title: Green Phase - Minimal Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Now write the minimal code needed to make the test pass. Do not add extra features or refactor. Just get the test to pass.
prompt_templates.scenario: Implementing only what's needed to pass the test

prompt_templates.title: TDD for Bug Fix
prompt_templates.description: Write a test that reproduces the bug before fixing it
prompt_templates.prompt: There's a bug where [describe bug]. First write a test that reproduces this bug and fails. Then I'll fix it.

output_examples.input: I need to add a function that validates email addresses
The login function should handle wrong passwords

best_practices: Write one test at a time - focus on single behavior
Name tests by the behavior they verify, not by what is being tested
Always watch the test fail before writing implementation
Keep implementation minimal - only pass the current test
anti_patterns: Writing implementation code before tests (then 'adapting' tests)
Keeping failed implementation code as 'reference' while writing tests
Tests that pass immediately - they prove nothing
Testing mock behavior instead of real component behavior

faq.question: What is Test-Driven Development?
faq.answer: TDD is a methodology where you write a failing test first, then write minimal code to pass it, then refactor. The cycle is: Red (fail), Green (pass), Refactor.

faq.question: Why write tests before code?
faq.answer: Tests written after code pass immediately, proving nothing. Writing tests first forces you to define desired behavior and proves the test actually catches bugs.

faq.question: What if the test is hard to write?
faq.answer: Difficulty writing tests often indicates the design is too complex. Simplify the interface or use dependency injection to make code testable.

faq.question: Can I skip TDD for simple code?
faq.answer: No. Simple code still breaks. The time to write a test is minimal, and it proves the code works and prevents future regressions.

faq.question: What if I already wrote the implementation?
faq.answer: Delete it. Start fresh with TDD. Keeping existing code and adding tests after defeats the purpose and gives false confidence.

faq.question: Does TDD slow down development?
faq.answer: No. TDD finds bugs before commit, prevents regressions, and enables safe refactoring. Debugging after the fact is slower than TDD.
